coredata.dc:title: Modification of polyacrylonitrile TFC-FO membrane by biowaste-derived hydrophilic N-doped carbon quantum dots for enhanced water desalination performance
coredata.dc:creator: Mazhari, Reza
Bide, Yasamin
Hosseini, Seyed Saeid
Shokrollahzadeh, Soheila
coredata.dc:description: Effective membranes are critical for enhancing the productivity of the forward osmosis (FO) desalination process. In this work, a straightforward approach is presented for constructing a hydrophilic and high-flux thin film composite FO (TFC-FO) membrane onto a polyacrylonitrile (PAN) sublayer. To this purpose, biowaste-derived nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (BWD-NCQDs) were incorporated into the polymeric structure of the TFC selective layer, which improved surface hydrophilicity and caused the creation of more water channels, resulting in better intrinsic transport properties. In both FO and pressure retarded osmosis (PRO) membrane orientations, TFC-FO membranes containing 0.01–0.1 wt% of BWD-NCQDs showed better water permeation and NaCl rejection than the pristine TFC membrane. In the presence of 0.05 wt% of BWD-NCQDs, water flux increased by 54.7 % and reached 24.6 Lm−2 h−1, and reverse solute flux decreased by 43.3 % to 3.81 g−2 h−1 compared to the pristine membrane. The optimum membrane also showed better fouling and water desalination behavior. Therefore, modification of the membranes by hydrophilic BWD-NCQDs represents superior performance in the FO processes. This study demonstrates the potential of using hydrophilic NCQDs derived from biowaste materials to incorporate into TFC membranes and improve their FO proficiency in water desalination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Forward osmosis membrane
Thin film composite
Hydrophilic N-doped carbon quantum dots
Banana biowaste

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assessment of transport properties of the fabricated membranes
Characterization of the BWD-NCQDs incorporated membranes
Performance of BWD-NCQDs-incorporated TFC membranes in forward osmosis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A Flow chart of TFC-FO membrane preparation.
TEM images (a–b) and particle size distribution histogram curve (c) of synthesized BWD-NCQDs.
ATR-FTIR spectrograms of membranes containing various loadings of BWD-NCQDs.
The SEM images of the TFC-FO membranes: (a) M-0, (b) M-0.025, (c) M-0.05, (d) M-0.075, and (e) M-0.1.
AFM images of TFC-FO membranes: (a) M-0, (b) M-0.025, (c) M-0.05, (d) M- 0.075, (e) M-0.1.
Water contact angle of the BWD-NCQDs-incorporated and pristine FO membranes.
(a) FO water flux and (b) Reverse salt flux of pristine and BWD-NCQDs-incorporated membranes (Feed solution: DI water; draw solution: 1 M NaCl).
The effect of DS concentration on FO performance of the BWD-NCQDs incorporated M-0.05 FO membranes (feed solution: DI water; draw solution: NaCl solution; membrane orientation: AL-FS mode).
Antifouling performance of TFC-FO membranes (M-0 and M-0.05) for 6 h with 30 min break for membrane washing (100 mgL−1 BSA and 2 M NaCl were used as aqueous feed and draw solutions, respectively).
Intrinsic transport properties of the synthesized TFC-FO membranes.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aRO test; Feed solution: DI water; Applied pressure: 3 bar.
b,cRO test; Feed solution: 1000 mgL−1 NaCl solution; Applied pressure: 3 bar.
dFO test in (AL-FS) mode; Feed solution: DI water; Draw solution: 0.5 M NaCl.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparing the performance of various FO membranes.

head.ce:title: Modification of polyacrylonitrile TFC-FO membrane by biowaste-derived hydrophilic N-doped carbon quantum dots for enhanced water desalination performance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology (IROST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effective membranes are critical for enhancing the productivity of the forward osmosis (FO) desalination process. In this work, a straightforward approach is presented for constructing a hydrophilic and high-flux thin film composite FO (TFC-FO) membrane onto a polyacrylonitrile (PAN) sublayer. To this purpose, biowaste-derived nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (BWD-NCQDs) were incorporated into the polymeric structure of the TFC selective layer, which improved surface hydrophilicity and caused the creation of more water channels, resulting in better intrinsic transport properties. In both FO and pressure retarded osmosis (PRO) membrane orientations, TFC-FO membranes containing 0.01–0.1 wt% of BWD-NCQDs showed better water permeation and NaCl rejection than the pristine TFC membrane. In the presence of 0.05 wt% of BWD-NCQDs, water flux increased by 54.7 % and reached 24.6 Lm−2 h−1, and reverse solute flux decreased by 43.3 % to 3.81 g−2 h−1 compared to the pristine membrane. The optimum membrane also showed better fouling and water desalination behavior. Therefore, modification of the membranes by hydrophilic BWD-NCQDs represents superior performance in the FO processes. This study demonstrates the potential of using hydrophilic NCQDs derived from biowaste materials to incorporate into TFC membranes and improve their FO proficiency in water desalination.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: pure water flux in RO testing mode (Lm−2 h−1)

ce:section.ce:para: The global deficit of clean water is a significant issue, even in areas with abundant water [1,2]. To address this problem, innovative, low-cost, and energy-efficient methods for water treatment that minimize the use of harmful materials are needed. The forward osmosis (FO) membrane processes have emerged as a promising method for water desalination compared to other membrane processes due to their low energy requirement, low fouling tendency, and acceptable water recovery [3–5]. Despite its advantages, FO membrane processes also present significant challenges, such as concentration polarization, low permeate flux, and relatively high reverse salt permeation, which must be addressed for better membrane performance [6].

A vast of activities has been done to develop high-quality FO membranes, using techniques such as thin-film composite [7], surface-modified membranes using layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly [8], and biomimetic membranes [9]. Among these methods, the fabrication of TFC membranes has received the most attention because both the sublayer and the selective layer can be manipulated to achieve the desired performance [10,11]. However, TFC membranes still face disadvantages, such as low water permeation, and high structure parameters contributing to internal concentration polarization (ICP) [4,12]. Researchers have used many nanoparticle additives to modify the selective layer of TFC membranes [10]. The use of nanoparticles such as zeolites [13], TiO2 [14], carbon nanotubes [15,16], graphene oxide [11,17], reduced graphene oxide [18], and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [19] has been shown to improve membrane performance.

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been used to improve FO membranes, mainly because of their rich surface in functional groups, small particle size, and narrow size distribution [20,21]. Bi et al. embedded CQD nanoparticles in TFC membranes, which resulted in four times higher water flux than pristine TFC membranes and remained stable over time [21]. Jain et al. fabricated TFC-FO membranes using manganese oxide (MnO2)-induced graphene quantum dots nanocomposites, resulting in higher porosity and reasonable salt rejection [22]. On the other hand, using nitrogen dopant carbonaceous material and its use in the membrane was initially based on molecular simulation by Gai and his colleagues and Chen and Yang [23,24]. Gai et al. proved that nitrogen-doped porous graphene can be simulated as a forward osmosis membrane. They found that the highly attractive energies present on the membrane surface are related to the negative charge of the pores, which leads to more reactions of ions and membrane pores and the movement of ions and water molecules [23]. Song and his colleagues showed that contamination with nitrogen limits the flux of ion particles with a hydraulic radius of less than 7.58 Å [25].

Despite the progress made in using CQDs in membranes, there are still challenges to be addressed, such as the availability of nanomaterial sources, simple methods of synthesizing nanoparticles with rich oxygen and amine functional groups, and their application to membranes. Using biowastes to synthesize carbon quantum dots is an efficient technique for preserving water resources without contamination [26]. Therefore, introducing hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots, derived from biowastes, into the TFC-FO membranes seems to be a practical solution to enhance the FO performance. In this work, BWD-NCQDs were synthesized using banana peel as a biowaste carbon source and applied to the interfacial polymerization (IP) as an additive. The simple synthetic procedure, availability of NCQDs resource, and hydrophilicity are some distinct advantages of the as-prepared NCQDs and FO membrane. For the first time, the BWD-NCQDs were incorporated into a TFC membrane based on a PAN sublayer to improve its performance for water desalination in the FO process.

Dwarf banana was prepared from the Sistan and Baluchestan province in Iran. Ammonia (25 % NH3 solution), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99 %), and n-hexane (97 %, anhydrous), 1,3-phenylenediamine (MPD, 99.5 %), and 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, 98 %) were received from Merck Co. Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 66.3 kDa) was purchased from MP Biomedical Co. Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) powder (MW: 90,000 gmol−1) was purchased from Polyacryl-Iran Co. Ltd. (Isfahan, Iran).

The BWD-NCQDs was synthesized from a biowaste carbon source (dwarf banana peel) using the hydrothermal technique (bottom-up route) based on Atchudan et al. procedure [27]. To prepare the banana peel suspension, 3.5 g of banana peel was effectively stirred in 70 mL of deionized water for 1 h. Then, 1 mL of 25 % ammonia was added to the suspension, which was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. The prepared solution was passed through a 0.22 μm polyethersulfone (PES) hydrophilic membrane after cooling, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for half an hour to remove dust particles and agglomerates. The resulting clear golden brownish BWD-NCQDs solution was reserved at 10 °C. Fig. 1 displays a flow chart of the preparation steps of TFC-FO membranes.

The support layer of the membrane was fabricated using the nonsolvent-induced phase separation (NIPS) method. The casting solution was prepared by mixing 18 wt% of PAN in DMF at 60 °C. The solution was degassed overnight and then cast onto glass using a casting knife with a thickness of 100 μm, followed by immersing it in deionized water (DI) at room temperature for phase inversion. The water in the coagulation bath was changed twice to remove excess solvent. The synthesized membranes were stored in DI water at ambient temperature. To establish hydrophilic groups on the membrane surface, the prepared PAN sublayers were hydrolyzed by soaking them in a 1.5 M sodium hydroxide solution (NaOH) at 45 °C for 1 h [11]. After hydrolysis, the sublayers were washed several times with DI water to reach neutral pH.

The IP method prepared the BWD-NCQDs-incorporated polyamide selective layer on the hydrolyzed PAN sublayer. In the first step of the IP process, the hydrolyzed PAN sublayer was immersed in a 2.0 wt% MPD aqueous solution, adding BWD-NCQDs at a concentration ranging from 0 to 0.1 wt%. The surplus of monomers was removed from the surface of the sublayer using a rubber ruler. In the second step, a 0.1 wt/v% TMC in n-hexane was poured on top of the membranes for 1 min to initiate interfacial polymerization and form a polyamide layer. Then, the membranes were washed with n-hexane solution to detach non-reacted TMC monomers and dried at room temperature for 1 min.

The functional groups of the synthesized BWD-NCQDs were examined using the spectroscopy technique. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy (Frontier, Perkin Elmer) was utilized (range: 4000–500 cm−1, resolution: 0.5 cm−1). The UV–visible spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer Lambda25 device, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Philips X'Pert MPD) was employed to study the crystal structures. Additionally, a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 F20 Super Twin TEM (accelerating voltage: 200 kV)) was used to analyze the elemental composition (energy dispersive X-ray (EDX)), morphology, and size distribution of the BWD-NCQDs. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Brookhaven 90Plus) method was also employed for the determination of size distribution. An ATR-FTIR spectroscopy technique (range: 4000–1000 cm−1, resolution: 0.5 cm−1) on dried samples was used for appraisal of the chemical properties of TFC membranes. The structure and roughness of the membrane's surface were tested using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN Vega) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Veeco CP II), respectively, with gold coating for SEM samples. The selective layer of the TFC membrane average thickness (Lav) was measured using Image J software from three different parts. The hydrophilicity of TFC membrane surfaces was assessed by measuring contact angle (Dataphysics OCA 15 plus) at room temperature, with each test repeated three times. Zeta potential analyzer (EKA Anton-Paar) has been utilized using 0.033 M of KCl solution (pH = 5.3) to measure the surface charge of membranes by the Fairbrother-Mastin method at 24 ± 1 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of transport properties of the fabricated membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of the fabricated FO membranes was evaluated in a cross-flow filtration apparatus (RO mode) by measuring pure water permeability coefficient (A, Lm−2h−1bar−1), NaCl rejection (R, %), and NaCl permeability coefficient (B, LMH) at a pressure of 3 bar and membrane area of 14 cm2. The pure water flux (J) and water permeability coefficient (A) of DI water were determined by Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.
(1)
J =
∆ V
A m
. ∆ t
(2)
A =
J
∆ P
where J is the pure water flux (Lm−2 h−1, LMH), ∆V is the volume change of permeate water (L), Am is the membrane effective area (m2), ∆t is the testing time of filtration (h), and ∆P (bar) is the transmembrane pressure [28]. A feed NaCl solution of 1000 mgL−1 was used for evaluation of the salt rejection (R) and calculated using Eq. (3). The feed and permeate concentrations were determined by conductivity measurement of the feed and permeate solutions and comparing them to a concentration-conductivity calibration curve.
(3)
R = 1 −
C p
C f
where
C f
and
C p
are the NaCl concentrations (M) in feed and permeate solutions, respectively. The salt permeability coefficient (B, LMH) was calculated by Eq. (4):
(4)
B =
1 R
− 1
J

A lab-scale plate and frame cross-flow setup was used to evaluate the FO efficiency of the TFC membranes (effective area: 1.5 cm × 1.8 cm). A schematic of the FO setup is shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). Deionized water and NaCl solution (1 M) were used as the feed and draw solutions, respectively. The desalination performance of TFC-FO membranes was investigated in AL-FS (FO) mode, using 0.6 M NaCl solution (a concentration similar to seawater) and 2 M NaCl as the draw solution. The flow velocity in both channels of the feed and draw solution sides was 1100 Lh−1. The FO performance was evaluated in two membrane orientations of AL-FS (selective layer-faced feed solution) and AL-DS (selective layer-faced draw solution) at room temperature (25° ± 1C) [29]. Before each FO examination, a 30-min pre-test was conducted to ensure steady-state conditions. The volume changes of the draw solution during the FO process were measured and recorded, and the average value from three replicate experiments was reported.

The permeated water flux (Jv) and the reverse salt flux (Js, gm−2 h−1, gMH) were computed by Eqs. (5) and (6).
(5)
J v
=
∆
V d
A m
. ∆ t
(6)
J S
=
∆
C f
V f
A m
. ∆ t
where ∆Vd is the volume change of draw solution (L), Δt is the testing time (h), Am is the membrane effective area (m2), Cf is the salt concentration (gL−1) and Vf is the feed solution volume (L).

Due to the presence of internal concentration polarization, an ICP model (Eq. (7)) was used for the calculation of structure parameter (S) in the AL-FS mode [29].
(7)
J V
=
D S
ln
A ∗
π D
+ B
A ∗
π F
+
J V
+ B
where D is the salt diffusion coefficient (m2s−1),
π D
and
π F
are the osmotic pressures of the draw and feed solutions (bar), respectively.

The fouling test in TFC membranes has been done in FO mode with 100 mgL−1 BSA aqueous solution as a fouling solution in the lab-scale cross-flow FO system at room temperature (25 ± 1 °C). At first, the FO system was stabilized by DI water on both sides of the membrane for 1 h. Then, the feed and draw solutions were replaced with 100 mgL−1 BSA aqueous solution and 2 M sodium chloride, respectively. Each fouling test lasts 3 h, and TFC membranes were washed for 30 min before every round to be prepared for the following sequence of tests.

In this research, BWD-NCQDs were synthesized and incorporated into the selective layer of TFC-FO membranes. The functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen in BWD-NCQDs are anticipated to improve the proficiency of the FO membrane. The characterization of the synthesized BWD-NCQDs, the intrinsic properties, and the efficiency of TFC-FO membranes was examined and analyzed to evaluate their potential for use in the water desalination process via forward osmosis.

The successful synthesis of the BWD-NCQDs was confirmed by ATR-FTIR, EDX, XRD, UV–vis, and TEM analyses. The ATR-FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 ) revealed the presence of surface-active functionalized groups at several spectra. The merged OH and NH stretching vibration bands were observed nearby 3400 cm−1 [30]. The observed stretching band of 2830 and 2950 cm−1 was of sp3 CH, and the carboxylic acid (C=O) stretching vibration was spotted around 1720 cm−1 [31]. The presence of aromatic rings was confirmed by stretching vibration of –C=C nearby 1590 cm−1 [32]. The CN stretching vibration at 1460 cm−1 demonstrates the N element on the BWD-NCQDs surface [33].

Additionally, some stretching vibrations of reasonable functional groups such as –COO, C–OH, and C–O–C was shown at 1250, 1180, and 1080 cm−1, respectively [34,35]. As shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information), the EDX spectrum analysis of BWD-NCQDs offers a variety of elements, such as carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), silicon (Si), chlorine (Cl), copper (Cu) and potassium (K), which is related to the intended and reasonable amount of C, O, and N elements in the synthesized BWD-NCQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S3-a, Supporting Information) revealed two transitions. The band around 281 nm is ascribed to the π–π* transitions of the aromatic CC and the band around 326 nm is assigned to n–π* transitions of the CO and CN bonds [36,37].

The XRD analysis of BWD-NCQDs confirmed the presence of a prominent peak around 22.6 (as seen in Fig. S3-b, Supporting Information), which is attributed to plane 002 with an interlayer space of approximately 0.37 nm [38]. The interlayer space of the 002 planes is larger than that of bulk graphite, demonstrating a graphitic structure of the synthesized BWD-NCQDs with some amorphous carbon [39]. This amorphous carbon is believed to be derived from the functional groups of the BWD-NCQDs surface [40].

As shown in Fig. 3 (a–b), the TEM images represent proper dispersion of the synthesized BWD-NCQDs in an aqueous solution with no noticeable agglomeration and spherical shape morphology. The particle size distribution histogram shows a restricted narrow range with an average size of 3.13 nm (Fig. 3c). In addition, the obtained DLS size distribution curve of BWD-NCQDs and TEM particle size marking figure were located in Supporting Information (Figs. S4 and S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the BWD-NCQDs incorporated membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The membranes incorporating various contents of BWD-NCQDs in the polyamide selective layer onto a hydrolyzed PAN sublayer were studied and characterized. The ATR-FTIR spectra of these membranes displayed two significant peaks, one for nitrile groups (–CN) at 2240 cm−1 and the other for carboxyl groups (–COOH) at 1730 cm−1 (as shown in Fig. 4 ). The nitrile groups were still present but with low intensity [41]. The intensity of carboxyl groups at 1730 cm−1 decreased continuously by adding BWD-NCQDs. The participation of BWD-NCQDs in the polyamide matrix resulted in a severe decline in most peaks [42,43]. The pristine M-0 membrane displayed clear amide group peaks at 1640 cm−1 (CO stretch of carbonyl) and 1526 cm−1 (NH bend) [44,45]. A slight shift in the two amide bond peaks indicated the impact of BWD-NCQDs. In the range of 1600 to 1700 cm−1, three peaks were observed at 1624 cm−1, around 1650 cm−1, and 1670 cm−1, which correlate with (NH stretch of amide), (CO stretch of carboxylic), and (CN of pyrrolic), respectively. Additionally, the broadband peak around 3300 cm−1 in BWD-NCQDs-incorporated membranes suggests an enhancement of –OH functionality and correspondingly improved surface hydrophilicity [46,47].

The cross-section and top surface of fabricated membranes were analyzed and demonstrated in Fig. 5 . The SEM images of the membranes' surfaces displayed the typical ridge-valley pattern that is a result of the IP reaction [48,49]. The cross-section of the membranes exhibited finger-like pores of the sublayer in combination with thin selective layers [50,51].

Incorporation of BWD-NCQDs into the membrane resulted in a thinner selective layer up to a specific loading (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). The spatial geometry of BWD-NCQDs reduced the rate of interfacial polymerization, which resulted in low MPD aqueous monomer penetration and created a thinner selective layer [52]. The lower thickness of the selective layer decreases the resistance to water passage. It reduces the structure parameter directly related to internal concentration polarization (ICP) as one of the serious challenges of FO membranes [12]. By adding more BWD-NCQDs, more water passage is allowed through the TFC-FO membranes. Higher loading of BWD-NCQDs increases the thickness of the selective layer, which leads to more pore blocking, nanoparticle aggregation, and BWD-NCQDs leakage that reduces water permeability [42,51].

The 3-D AFM images of membrane surfaces, as shown in Fig. 6 , indicate an increase in roughness and higher amounts of BWD-NCQDs. This increase in roughness is expected to increase water flux, which can increase the amount of surface area available in contact with water molecules due to the larger specific surface area. This can be attributed to the linkage of aqueous monomer (MPD) to BWD-NCQDs, resulting in a larger structure that decreases the MPD diffusion, leading to a sharper swelling and outcurve morphology [15,53]. Although, drastic changes in surface roughness are not observed in this study due to the preparation of nanoparticles with uniform distribution and the absence of noticeable clumping of nanoparticles in higher concentrations [54].

The wettability of the membrane surface was assessed and is presented in Fig. 7 . It was observed that as the concentration of BWD-NCQDs in the selective layer of the membrane increased, the membrane surface showed higher hydrophilicity. The contact angle of the membrane decreased from 61.7° in the pristine TFC (M-0) to 36.3° when 0.1 wt% of BWD-NCQDs nanoparticles was added to the membrane (M-0.1). Due to the oxygen functional groups of the BWD-NCQDs nanoparticles (especially the improvement of –OH functionality of TFC-FO membranes by adding BWD-NCQDs as shown in ATR-FTIR spectrum), more water was attracted. More water nano-channels were created, which could be responsible for increasing the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface, leading to improved water permeability of the membrane [55,56]. A more hydrophilic surface also improves the anti-adhesion of foulant and the antifouling propensity of TFC-FO membranes [57].

Electrostatic repulsion is one of the noteworthy elements in salt separation that depends on the interaction of negative charges of the membrane surface and negative ions and particles in the feed solution [58]. The zeta potential of the hydrolyzed sublayer (H-PAN) surface was measured as −11.11 mV, which is corresponded to carboxyl groups that were formed during hydrolysis by NaOH solution [41]. M-0 and M-0.05 TFC membranes showed less surface charge than H-PAN, −4.58 mV and −7.15 mV, respectively. Owning more hydrophilic surface groups in M-0.05, the surface charge is more negative than M-0. This negative surface charge of the membrane is beneficial for ions rejection.

The intrinsic characteristics of membranes containing BWD-NCQDs are displayed in Table 1 . The water permeability coefficient (A) of TFC membranes remarkably increased by incorporating of BWD-NCQDs. This improvement in water permeability coefficient can be attributed to two main factors. Firstly, the insertion of BWD-NCQDs in the polyamide selective layer enhanced the hydrophilicity of the surface of the TFC membrane, which led to an increase in water transport through the membrane's water channels [47]. Secondly, the use of BWD-NCQDs in the IP process resulted in a thinner selective layer than the pristine TFC membrane (M-0), which also contributing to the increase in water permeability coefficient. The BWD-NCQDs-incorporated TFC-FO membranes exhibited higher salt rejection than the pristine TFC membrane. Despite the enhancement in salt rejection up to 0.05 wt% of BWD-NCQDs, a slight decrease in rejection was observed in M-0.075 and M-0.1. The formation of mini-defects in the selective layer at higher concentrations of BWD-NCQDs due to agglomeration during the IP process could be responsible for the decrement in rejection [15]. Additionally, the salt permeability coefficient, which has an inverse relationship with salt rejection, initially decreased up to 0.05 wt% of BWD-NCQDs and then increased with the addition of more BWD-NCQDs. Generally, a lower B/A ratio represents better membrane selectivity and is approvable for FO performance [49,59]. The best membrane selectivity was obtained for M-0.05 and the B/A ratio was measured as 0.11, which was the lowest value compared to other synthesized TFC-FO membranes.

The structure parameter (S) is an essential factor in determining the degree of ICP in TFC membranes, as per the classical ICP model established by Loeb et al. A lower S value correlates with lower ICP and improved forward osmosis performance [60,61]. The S values of the TFC-FO membranes synthesized in this study are presented in Table 1. The S value of the pristine TFC membrane (M-0) is 311 μm, which decreases by 30.2 % and 44.0 % for M-0.025 and M-0.05, respectively, when BWD-NCQDs are incorporated.

However, as the BWD-NCQDs loading increases, the S value and the degree of ICP may also increase. This increase in S value may be due to an overall increase in membrane thickness and the impact of transport properties such as A and B values [62]. The M-0.05 membrane exhibited high water permeability coefficient, good selectivity and salt rejection, in addition to lower S value, which makes it a suitable candidate for improving FO performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of BWD-NCQDs-incorporated TFC membranes in forward osmosis
ce:section.ce:para: The forward osmosis performance of the TFC membranes incorporating BWD-NCQDs was evaluated, and the results are presented in Fig. 8 . As shown in Fig. 8a, the incorporation of BWD-NCQDs in the membranes led to an increase in water flux in both orientations, when compared to the pristine TFC membrane. Additionally, the reverse salt flux also decreased in all BWD-NCQDs-embedded TFC membranes compared to the pristine one (Fig. 8b). As expected, the M-0.05 membrane exhibited the best FO performance compared to all the membranes tested. This preferable performance can be ascribed to the decreased average thickness of the selective layer and increased hydrophilicity of the membrane surface, which was achieved by the incorporation of surface functional groups during the IP reaction, resulting in the formation of new and efficient water pathways for better water flux transport [63].

Furthermore, the decrease in reverse salt flux also indicates a good agreement with the salt rejection results reported by other researchers [64]. Despite the enhancement in water flux and reverse salt flux up to 0.05 wt% BWD-NCQDs loading in all TFC membranes, their efficiency decreased when extra loading of BWD-NCQDs was added in M-0.075 and M-0.1 membranes. This decrease in FO water and reverse salt flux was also observed in intrinsic properties. It can be attributed to the aggregation of BWD-NCQDs, which leads to the blocking of surface pores and water channels, making water transport in TFC membranes less efficient. The M-0.05 was selected for further FO performance tests due to its better FO performance in water and reverse salt flux than the other TFC-FO membranes.

A comparison between intrinsic transport properties and FO performance of different reported membranes is presented in Table 2 . The Js/Jv ratio of the present study is low, which can be attributed to the effective formation of a selective polyamide layer, which leads to a good salt rejection. Additionally, the high water permeability coefficient of this study is due to the rich hydrophilic functional groups and water pathways in the TFC membranes, resulting in higher water flux. Furthermore, the structure parameter of this work was effectively controlled to minimize resistance to water transport through the membrane.

In Fig. 9 , the effect of DS concentration on FO efficiency was examined by observing the water flux and reverse salt flux of the M-0.05 membrane in AL-FS mode. Both water and reverse salt fluxes showed a similar increasing trend as the draw solution concentration increased. This increase is ascribed to the higher osmotic driving force provided by the more concentrated draw solution.

However, despite the increased water flux with higher draw solution concentration, the reverse salt flux increased slightly at NaCl concentrations higher than 1.5 M. The Js/Jv value using 0.5, 1, and 1.5 M NaCl draw solution reached 0.20, 0.154, and 0.153, respectively. For draw solution concentrations greater than 1.5 M, the Js/Jv had the lowest value of 0.12 and 0.1 for 2 and 3 M NaCl draw solution, respectively, and could be used for further FO investigations.

The desalination performance of the optimum M-0.05 membrane (feed solution: 0.6 M NaCl, draw solution: 2 M NaCl) showed an average water flux of 37.3 LMH, which is considerably higher than the water flux of M-0 (i.e. 20.7 LMH). These results indicated the improvement of the synthesized TFC-FO membrane in water desalination.

As shown in Fig. 10 , the anti-fouling properties of TFC-FO membranes were obtained in AL-FS mode during the 15 h. Membrane orientation AL-DS causes more fouling due to the accumulation of more foulants in the porous sublayer of TFC-FO, for this reason, AL-FS mode was used in this research [70]. With the addition of BSA foulant particles in the feed solution, a decrease in the water flux passing through the membrane was observed.

The M-0.05 membrane showed a lower water flux reduction during the fouling runs than M-0. Also, after washing the two membranes, the water recovery reached 98.8 % in M-0.05, which was 7.8 % higher than M-0. These results are consistent with the outcomes of zeta potential analysis and indicate that the interactions between the surface-charged groups of the membrane and negative ions in the fouling solution and irreversible fouling have been minimized in the TFC-FO membrane.

In this work, the TFC-FO membranes with BWD-NCQDs incorporated into the selective layer were fabricated on a hydrolyzed PAN sublayer via interfacial polymerization. The BWD-NCQDs, synthesized from the banana peel as a green biowaste, contained active and rich amine and oxygen functional groups on their surface with an average size of 3.13 nm. The ATR-FTIR spectra showed that BWD-NCQDs were effectively incorporated into the polymeric structure and improved the interfacial polymerization reaction. The water flux and reverse salt flux of TFC membranes with different BWD-NCQDs loadings were higher than that of the pristine TFC membrane in both membrane orientations. The TFC-FO membrane with 0.05 wt% BWD-NCQDs amount showed a hydrophilic surface and good FO performance compared to other TFC membranes. The Js/Jv value of M-0.05 was 0.154, which is a good indicator of FO membrane selectivity. The structure parameter reached 174 μm in M-0.05 that corresponds to the presence of more water channels in the FO membrane and minimal water resistance. However, increasing the BWD-NCQDs loading led to a non-uniform selective layer surface and nanoparticle aggregation, which negatively impacted FO membrane efficiency. The fouling performance of the synthesized membrane showed almost reversible fouling. These findings indicate the potential of incorporating BWD-NCQDs in TFC-FO membranes to improve their performance.
R. Mazhari: Investigation, Writing original draft.
Y. Bide: Analytical methodology, Validation, Writing review and editing.
S. Shokrollahzadeh: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing review and editing.
S. S. Hosseini: Supervision, Writing review and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2023.116888.
